refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is bounded by customer-negotiated network conditions and acceptance criteria, making it measurable and verifiable.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across Telemetry (feedback) and Tasking (command) paths. Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit (default 1%) sustained over representative operational duration.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands (Tasking) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle telemetry (Telemetry) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication paths (dual network links) with automatic failover to maintain packet loss below threshold.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and packet retransmission logic to minimize loss on primary link.
DP-C: Implement forward error correction (FEC) and packet aggregation to reduce effective packet loss impact.
DP-D: Combine redundant paths (DP-A) with QoS/retransmission (DP-B) and FEC (DP-C) in a layered resilience architecture.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a layered teleoperation resilience architecture combining (1) redundant communication paths with automatic failover, (2) adaptive QoS prioritization and selective retransmission for critical packets (Tasking, SafetyAlert), and (3) forward error correction on primary link to reduce packet loss impact below customer threshold under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation resilience to network infrastructure design (Network Link interface availability and characteristics).
DP-D couples to Safety Services for SafetyAlert generation when packet loss exceeds threshold.
DP-D couples to Vehicle for Telemetry collection and Tasking command execution timing.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are expected and manageable via interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No hidden or problematic couplings detected. DP-D is internally coherent: redundancy, QoS, and FEC are complementary strategies, not conflicting.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment environment. Risk: If actual network differs significantly, packet loss targets may not be achievable.
Assumption: Redundant network paths (dual links) are available and can be provisioned. Risk: Cost and infrastructure constraints may limit redundancy options.
Assumption: Retransmission and FEC overhead (latency, bandwidth) are acceptable for teleoperation use case. Risk: Excessive latency from retransmission may degrade operator control responsiveness.
Assumption: SafetyAlert interface can be triggered reliably even under degraded communication. Risk: If communication is severely degraded, safety alerts themselves may not transmit reliably.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define packet loss detection thresholds, failover trigger conditions, and SafetyAlert escalation logic. Specify acceptable latency bounds for retransmission and FEC to ensure operator control responsiveness is not compromised. Validate that SafetyAlert path has independent or higher-priority transmission guarantee.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication resilience logic for teleoperation, including redundant path management, QoS prioritization, packet retransmission, forward error correction, and communication health monitoring. It interfaces with Vehicle (Telemetry, Tasking), Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and network infrastructure (Network Link).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives vehicle state telemetry from Vehicle module. Teleoperation module monitors packet loss on this inbound stream and applies FEC/redundancy to ensure delivery to tele-operator.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Transmits operator commands from tele-operator to Vehicle module. Teleoperation module applies QoS prioritization and retransmission to ensure command delivery with low packet loss.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits communication degradation alerts (packet loss exceeding threshold) to Safety Services. Teleoperation module triggers SafetyAlert when packet loss is detected above customer threshold.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages dual network paths (primary and backup) for teleoperation traffic. Teleoperation module controls failover logic and monitors link health.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of packet loss resilience logic. All core DP-D functions (redundancy, QoS, retransmission, FEC, health monitoring) are cohesive and belong in this module. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert generation logic may be shared with Safety Services, but triggering logic resides in Teleoperation.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-side telemetry collection and command execution, but should not own communication resilience logic. Placing DP-D in Vehicle would scatter network-layer concerns across vehicle firmware, creating tight coupling and diffusion.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alert generation, but communication resilience is a network/QoS concern, not a safety policy concern. Placing DP-D in Safety Services would conflate safety logic with network engineering.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container, but DP-D is a subsystem-level concern best owned by Teleoperation. Assigning to NGHE Solution would bypass architectural layering and create diffusion across multiple subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (layered resilience architecture). It has clear ownership of teleoperation communication paths, interfaces with Vehicle (Telemetry, Tasking) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert), and can manage Network Link redundancy without diffusion. Cohesion is high: all resilience functions (redundancy, QoS, retransmission, FEC) are tightly related and belong together.
containment_risks: Risk: If Network Link interface is not well-defined or if network infrastructure is managed by a separate team, Teleoperation module may lack authority to control failover and link selection. Mitigation: Establish clear Network Link interface contract and ownership boundaries.

Risk: SafetyAlert generation logic may be duplicated if Safety Services also monitors communication health independently. Mitigation: Establish single source of truth: Teleoperation detects packet loss and triggers SafetyAlert; Safety Services consumes and acts on alert.

Risk: Telemetry and Tasking interfaces may have latency or ordering guarantees that conflict with retransmission/FEC overhead. Mitigation: Validate interface contracts for acceptable latency bounds and packet ordering semantics.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has explicit ownership of Network Link failover logic and that network infrastructure team supports dual-path provisioning. Define SafetyAlert interface contract to ensure Teleoperation is the sole source of communication health alerts. Validate Telemetry and Tasking interface latency budgets to ensure retransmission/FEC overhead does not violate operator control responsiveness requirements.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (layered resilience architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-009 (packet loss ≤ customer threshold). The combination of redundant paths, QoS/retransmission, and FEC provides multiple mechanisms to achieve the target. DP-D is implementable and measurable. No logical gaps between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion risk: SafetyAlert generation logic may be duplicated if Safety Services independently monitors communication health. Mitigation: Establish single source of truth in Teleoperation module.

Diffusion risk: Network Link failover logic may be scattered across Teleoperation and network infrastructure layers if interface contract is unclear. Mitigation: Define explicit Network Link interface and ownership boundaries.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment score (95) and low diffusion score (5). All core DP-D functions are cohesive and belong in Teleoperation. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and manageable. No architectural packaging issues detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via packet loss measurement under representative network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics and SafetyAlert triggers for testing. Acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss) are measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns packet loss resilience. Vehicle module owns telemetry/command execution. Safety Services owns alert consumption and safety policy enforcement. Interfaces are explicit (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). No ambiguity in responsibility allocation.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify packet loss detection thresholds, failover trigger conditions, and SafetyAlert escalation logic.
Define Network Link interface contract to clarify failover authority and dual-path provisioning requirements.
Validate Telemetry and Tasking interface latency budgets to ensure retransmission/FEC overhead is acceptable.
Establish single source of truth for communication health monitoring: Teleoperation detects and reports via SafetyAlert; Safety Services consumes and acts.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and has been decomposed into three sub-FRs (command transmission, telemetry transmission, communication degradation detection). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection at the next level. DP-D is sound and well-allocated to Teleoperation module, but implementation details (redundancy strategy, QoS algorithm, FEC scheme, failover logic) must be elaborated in sub-level decompositions. Proceed to next level to refine sub-FRs and define detailed DPs for each.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator commands (Tasking) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle telemetry (Telemetry) with packet loss ≤ customer threshold.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss exceeding threshold) via SafetyAlert.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are stable and representative of actual deployment.
Redundant network paths (dual links) are available and can be provisioned.
Retransmission and FEC overhead (latency, bandwidth) are acceptable for teleoperation use case.
SafetyAlert interface can be triggered reliably even under degraded communication.
Teleoperation module has authority to manage Network Link failover.
Safety Services does not independently monitor communication health (single source of truth).
open_risks: Network infrastructure may not support dual-path provisioning or failover control.
Retransmission/FEC latency overhead may degrade operator control responsiveness.
SafetyAlert path itself may not be reliable under severe communication degradation.
Packet loss detection thresholds and failover trigger conditions not yet defined.
Telemetry and Tasking interface latency budgets not yet validated against DP-D overhead.
evidence_needed: Network infrastructure assessment: confirm dual-path availability and failover capability.
Latency budget analysis: validate that retransmission/FEC overhead does not exceed operator control responsiveness requirements.
SafetyAlert reliability analysis: confirm that safety alerts can be transmitted reliably even under degraded communication.
Packet loss measurement under representative network conditions: validate that DP-D achieves ≤1% packet loss target.
Interface contract definitions: Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link latency and ordering guarantees.
